title: Myths, Fables, and Hard Truths About AI Governance | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: CFR president Michael Froman uses the U.S. export-control ban on Anthropic's 'Mythos-class' models (including consumer Claude Fable 5) to argue AI safety is now a national-security matter, that the U.S. lacks any comprehensive AI law and is governing via ad hoc export controls, and that wielding frontier models as a chokepoint risks fragmenting the global AI landscape.

insights.claim: The U.S. government invoked export controls to bar any foreign national, including foreign-national Anthropic employees, from using Mythos-class models including the consumer version Claude Fable 5, amounting to a de facto blanket ban.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Treating a frontier model as an export-controlled good sets precedent that could freeze foreign access to U.S. AI overnight, a regulatory tail risk for AI-dependent businesses.
insights.quote: any foreign national, whether inside or outside the United States, including foreign national Anthropic employees
insights.locator: Earlier this month, the U.S. government invoked export controls

insights.claim: Enforcing the U.S.-citizen-only carve-out for a widely distributed software product is described as nearly impossible, especially since some Anthropic employees who build and maintain Mythos-class models are foreign nationals.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Export-control tools designed for physical goods map poorly onto distributed software, implying enforcement gaps and legal uncertainty.
insights.locator: Rapidly enforcing the distinctions of such a policy

insights.claim: The proximate trigger of the ban is disputed: candidates include Anthropic sharing powerful model versions with a South Korean telecom without White House permission, an escalation of the Trump administration's dispute with the company, and third-party jailbreaking of the model for dangerous cyber uses.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Multiple plausible triggers show how unpredictable and discretionary AI enforcement has become, raising compliance risk for model developers.
insights.quote: We may never know which straw broke the Claude camel’s back
insights.locator: The proximate cause of this de-facto ban is hotly debated

insights.claim: The Pentagon criticized Anthropic for trying to put guardrails around the military's use of Claude for fully autonomous weapons and mass domestic surveillance.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Government pushback against vendor-imposed use limits signals the state, not the company, will set permissible AI uses, reshaping AI vendors' control over their products.
insights.quote: fully autonomous weapons and mass domestic surveillance
insights.locator: started with Anthropic’s attempt to put guardrails around the Pentagon’s use

insights.claim: Froman argues AI governance has reached a historical tipping point where AI safety is no longer abstract and the latest models are being treated as potential national security threats.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Reframing frontier models as security threats invites heavier, slower-moving state intervention across the AI sector.
insights.quote: the latest AI models are being treated as potential national security threats
insights.locator: It is more important to recognize we have reached a historical tipping point

insights.claim: The central policy question is how to manage the trade-off between AI safety concerns and progress, particularly in the context of competition with China.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: The safety-vs-progress-vs-China trilemma will shape every major AI regulatory decision; positioning depends on which leg policymakers prioritize.
insights.locator: The question is how to manage the trade-off

insights.claim: Froman warns that slow-moving regulatory processes could have serious implications for the AI investment frenzy, which is underwritten by the assumption of rapid AI adoption.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: If regulation throttles adoption speed, the core thesis behind elevated AI valuations weakens, a direct flag for AI-exposed equities.
insights.quote: this development could have serious implications for the AI investment frenzy, which is underwritten by the assumption of rapid AI adoption
insights.locator: Given the slow pace at which regulatory processes tend to move

insights.claim: There are still no comprehensive federal laws regulating AI in the United States, leaving the government to act through ad hoc legal contortions like export controls and supply-chain risk designations.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Absent a legislative framework, AI rules arrive as unpredictable enforcement actions rather than stable law, raising regulatory risk premia.
insights.quote: There still aren’t any comprehensive federal laws regulating AI in the United States.
insights.locator: the Fable 5 ban revealed that the current domestic regulatory paradigm

insights.claim: One issue is settled: it will be the government, not companies, that determines whether a model or a particular use of a model is too dangerous.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: State control over what counts as too-dangerous strips AI firms of product-gatekeeping power, a structural shift in the industry's autonomy.
insights.quote: It will be the government, not companies, that determine whether a model, or a particular use of a model, is too dangerous.
insights.locator: One issue appears to be settled

insights.claim: The current paradigm leaves private AI industry exposed to policymaker volatility with few constraints on state intervention and no certainty about its nature.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Unbounded, unpredictable intervention risk is a discount factor on AI-company valuations and a deterrent to capital commitment.
insights.quote: leaves private industry vulnerable to the volatility of policymakers
insights.locator: The current paradigm leaves private industry vulnerable

insights.claim: Froman argues the costs of failing to solve AI governance—potential cyber attacks or slower productivity growth—will compound over time.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Framing inaction as a compounding cost argues for early regulatory clarity; the downside cases (cyber risk, lost productivity) bracket the macro stakes.
insights.quote: just because a problem doesn’t have a clear solution doesn’t mean it’s not a problem

themes.theme: Frontier models as a geopolitical chokepoint

insights.claim: Froman likens U.S. control of frontier AI access to China and rare-earth magnets or Iran and the Strait of Hormuz—a critical chokepoint Washington can open or close at will.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Casting U.S. frontier models as a weaponizable chokepoint reframes AI access as geopolitical leverage, with allies as the targets.
insights.quote: the United States has reaffirmed its control of a critical chokepoint it can open or close as it sees fit
insights.locator: Like China and magnets or Iran and the Strait of Hormuz

insights.claim: Foreign users now face a choice between building on U.S. models they could lose access to at any time or using domestic and open-source models, largely from China, that are a step behind the cutting edge and carry additional risks.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Access uncertainty pushes foreign demand toward Chinese and open models, a tailwind for Chinese AI ecosystems and a risk to U.S. platform lock-in.
insights.quote: building with models they could lose access to at any time
insights.locator: those outside the United States will face a choice

insights.claim: Froman predicts the ban creates a trade-off between embedding allies in the U.S. tech stack and the risk of sharing the most powerful technologies, with countries likely opting for AI sovereignty over serfdom.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: A push toward AI sovereignty could accelerate fragmentation of the global AI landscape, slow growth, and undermine the U.S. goal of defining global AI architecture.
insights.quote: Other countries may well opt for AI sovereignty over serfdom
insights.locator: The United States has sought to embed as many allies

insights.claim: Froman notes Andrea Miotti proposed an International Treaty to Ban Superintelligence as a draconian solution—making the most powerful models illegal to build in the first place.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: A treaty-to-ban proposal signals that prohibition-style governance is entering serious discourse, a far-tail policy scenario for frontier AI.
insights.locator: One rather draconian solution to all these problems
